fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.

Let f , g \colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}Bx be two dependent functions. A homotopy from f to g, writing H\colon f \sim g is given by a dependent function H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}fx=gx. We shall write 1_{f}\colon f \sim f for the identity homotopy on f defined by 1_{f}x:\equiv \mathsf {refl}.

Let s \colon B \to A and r \colon A \to B be a pair of maps. A homotopy h \colon r\circ s \sim 1_{B} exhibits s as a section of r and r as a retraction of s. Together, \mathopen {}\left ({s,r}\right )\mathclose {} are referred to as a section-retraction pair.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{B} and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{A}, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon , we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.

An inverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f\circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with an inverse as an invertible function.
A function f\colon A\to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x=y\to fx=fy is invertible.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We may define a path object structure on the type of reflexive graphs with vertices in a given type A, so that an isomorphism from \mathcal {G} to \mathcal {G}' is given by a pair of a pointwise equivalence of types H\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathopen {}\left ({x\approx _{\mathcal {G}}y}\right )\mathclose {}\cong \mathopen {}\left ({x\approx _{\mathcal {G}'}y}\right )\mathclose {} that preserves reflexivity data as witnessed by a dependent homotopy I\colon \mathopen {}\left ({\lambda {x}\mathpunct {.} H\,x\,x\,\mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}}{x}}\right )\mathclose {}}\right )\mathclose {} \sim \mathsf {rx}_{\mathcal {G}'}.

We first show that the dependent product of intrinsic path objects \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({-xx}\right )\mathclose {} is a left-cloven over the path object \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}: given an equivalence H : \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {G}}}\right )\mathclose {}\approx _{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}} \mathopen {}\left ({\approx _{\mathcal {G}'}}\right )\mathclose {} of graphs and a reflexivity datum r\colon \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}} x\approx _{\mathcal {G}} x, we define H_*r x :\equiv H\,x\,x\,\mathopen {}\left ({rx}\right )\mathclose {}; the unit law holds definitionally.

Therefore, we may form the covariant sum \mathchoice {\textstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \oint _{\mathopen {}\left ({E:\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x,y:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathsf {Type}}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }\mathopen {}\left ({Exx}\right )\mathclose {}, in which equivalences are given by homotopies of reflexivity data over graph equivalences.

Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g\colon A \to B. For any identification p\colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {act}_{g}\,p = \mathsf {act}_{f}\,p\cdot Hy.
By identification induction on p, it suffices to show that Hx\cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl}\cdot Hx.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{A} be a homotopy from f\colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H\circ f to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ H.

Fixing x:A, we must construct an identification from H\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} to \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({H x}\right )\mathclose {}. By the naturality () of H at the identification H x\colon f x = x, we have an identification H\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} \cdot \mathsf {act}_{1_{A}}\mathopen {}\left ({H x}\right )\mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({H x}\right )\mathclose {}\cdot H x.

Simplifying, we have H\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {}\cdot H x = \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({H x}\right )\mathclose {}\cdot H x; canceling H x on either side, we therefore have the desired identification H\mathopen {}\left ({f x}\right )\mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathopen {}\left ({H x}\right )\mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

As we evidently have \mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}{s}}\right )\mathclose {}^*h x = \mathsf {act}_{f}\mathsf {refl}\cdot h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of f\circ -\sim 1_{B} over the path object \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . In this sense, sectionhood witnesses are right-cloven over their underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

As we have \mathopen {}\left ({\mathsf {rx}_{\mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}\mathord {\vartriangle }A}r}\right )\mathclose {}^*h x = h x, the data above constitute a cleaving of -\circ f\sim 1_{A} over \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:B}\right )\mathclose {}}} \mathord {\vartriangle }A, both with their standard path object structure as given by (dependent) homotopies in . Thus retractionhood witnesses constitute are right-cloven over underlying maps.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The reflexive graph structure on \mathchoice {\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\textstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{\scriptscriptstyle \prod _{\mathopen {}\left ({x:A}\right )\mathclose {}}}{Bx} by homotopy and identity homotopies is univalent.
